name: Esperanto
notes: Esperanto — constructed international language (Zamenhof, 1887). Perfectly regular phonemic orthography: one letter = one sound. 28-letter alphabet with 6 diacritic letters (circumflex: Ĉ/Ĝ/Ĥ/Ĵ/Ŝ; breve: Ŭ). Stress always on penultimate syllable. J = /j/ (semivowel); Ĵ = /ʒ/. Ŭ = /w/ (semivowel in diphthongs aŭ, eŭ). The most widely spoken constructed language.

sources.author: Zamenhof, L.L.
sources.title: Unua Libro (An International Language)
sources.notes: The founding document establishing the 28-letter alphabet with a strict one-letter-one-sound correspondence.

sources.author: Zamenhof, L.L.
sources.title: Fundamento de Esperanto
sources.pages: Fundamenta Gramatiko, section A (Alphabet, grapheme-to-sound table) and Rule 10 (stress always on the penultimate syllable)
sources.notes: The prescriptive, immutable norm (netusxebla) codifying the phonemic orthography and fixed penultimate stress. Canonical, rule-numbered source for the grapheme-to-IPA mapping in this spec.

sources.author: Wells, J.C.
sources.title: Lingvistikaj aspektoj de Esperanto
sources.pages: Chapter on phonetics and phonology (fonetiko kaj fonologio)
sources.notes: Phonetician's descriptive account of the Esperanto phoneme inventory and its usual IPA values, including the semivowel realisations of j and u-breve.

stress.notes: Esperanto has perfectly regular penultimate stress. The stress rule is part of the Fundamento de Esperanto (Zamenhof 1905). No exceptions exist in native vocabulary. Source: Wells (1989), Lingvistikaj aspektoj de Esperanto; Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Esperanto_phonology
